text.content:
Read this before running Phase 3. Do not read aloud.

Phase 3 — Harbor Before Throne

Phase 3 is the middle link in the Loftwick → Hardby → Rel Astra chain. The party arrives in a city that is less restrained than Loftwick: Hardby is a Free City of merchants and mercenaries on the Wild Coast, governed by an old female-led council called the Gynarchy whose authority over the harbor district has been quietly slipping for two decades. The conspiracy's middle ring is operational here.

Phase 3 should accomplish four things, in roughly this order:

  • Name the broker. "Tarsh" — the Hardby intermediary who hired Merev Sarth — is identified by his real name and station, and confronted.
  • Resolve Tamsin Moraven's fate. Alive (rescued, with caveats), alive-but-fled-further-east (intel only), or confirmed dead (a body and a smuggled letter). Which outcome the table sees is determined by the travel approach the party chose at the end of Phase 2.
  • Get the senior partners of Cindren & Vhal Rel Astra inside the conspiracy by their own hand. Through Veska Maelan (the trade-clearing notary) and the C&V Hardby branch's own ledgers, the party recovers a document or document-pair that names a senior partner in writing. Not Veshen Cindren (the public-facing nephew); the recluse, Tarlith Vhal Sereth.
  • Clarify what "HARBOR BEFORE THRONE" operationally means. Phase 2 left the phrase as a slogan-of-intent. Phase 3 reveals it is timing law — the conspiracy is positioning to seat its people on Rel Astra's harbor commission before the next Aerdy Overking is named, so that whoever wins the Aerdy succession finds a Cindren-aligned harbor council waiting in Rel Astra, controlling the eastern revenue. Phase 3 does NOT yet identify which Aerdy claimant the conspirators support, or who the candidate harbor commissioners are. That is Phase 4.

The phase ends when the party knows the following, in their bones:

The conspiracy is older than Vandrell, larger than Cindren & Vhal, and timed to a succession that has not yet happened. The eastern commission is no longer a question. We go to Rel Astra now.

Travel approach matters here

Whichever travel approach the party chose at the end of Phase 2 substantively changes what Phase 3 looks like. This is the deliberate payoff of that choice — not a punishment for picking "wrong," but a different shape of investigation depending on visibility.

ApproachTime elapsed since the conspiracy was warnedTamsin Moraven outcomeVeska Maelan availabilityTarsh availability
Official Delegation~2 weeks (slow paperwork; firm has had time)Confirmed dead. Body recovered from harbor 3 days before party arrives.Has fled the city. Sympathetic former apprentice can pass party her notes.Has been moved upcountry; party gets to him via paper trail only, no confrontation.
Commercial Cover~1 week (firm noticed but hasn't confirmed)Alive but moved. Held in a coastal estate two days east of Hardby. Recoverable with effort.Alive in Hardby. Cautious. Will help if the party shows Caelith's letter in private.Alive in Hardby. Confronted in person; gives names if cornered.
Quiet Pursuit~3 days (party arrives ahead of the firm's suspicion)Alive, still in Hardby, held in a warehouse on Black Cog Lane. Live recovery possible.Alive in Hardby, fully cooperative, eager.Alive in Hardby, unprepared. Easiest confrontation.

Each row is a complete viable Phase 3. None is a failure state. The Official Delegation route gives the party legal protection and a slower, sadder, document-driven story. The Quiet Pursuit route gives them the highest-impact action sequences but no backstop if compromised. The Commercial Cover route is the middle and the recommended.

Tone

Hardby is not Loftwick. The Yeomanry is principled and being maneuvered around. Hardby is mercantile and being maneuvered through. Tone for Phase 3:

  • More dangerous, more visible. Street violence happens here. Mercenary squads work for hire openly. The harbor watch is unevenly bought.
  • Wealthier and seedier in the same neighborhoods. The merchants' quarter is rich. The harbor district is loud and uncautious. Cindren & Vhal Hardby's two-story building sits on a street where you can buy fresh fish, a stolen chronometer, and a contracted assassination within the same block.
  • The Gynarchy is real but tired. Hardby's elected council of matriarchs still rules, formally; in practice many of the city's daily decisions are made by guild captains and counting-house heads who do not bother to consult them. The conspiracy benefits from this distraction.
  • Caelith is not present. The party is on commission, writing back to him by sealed post. His brass token (the Hand of the Duke insignia) opens no doors here directly — but Veska Maelan respects it, and certain Hardby civic officials remember what it means.
  • Trina is not present either. Her letter to Castrian Vell functions in her stead if the party is on the Commercial Cover route.

Alicia note: the Tasha thread does not advance in Phase 3 unless the GM wants it to. Trina's mentor presence is by letter only. If Alicia's player invites a private letter beat, Trina sends one — short, kind, asking how the moths are.

What this phase does NOT resolve

  • The mastermind. "E." remains a single initial. Eclavdra is never named. Drow are never named.
  • The Aerdy succession. The party learns the conspiracy is timed to a succession; they do not yet learn which claimant the conspirators support, or who the candidate harbor commissioners are.
  • Veshen Cindren. The public-facing nephew at C&V Rel Astra. He may or may not be in the conspiracy; Phase 3 does not commit either way. The senior partner Phase 3 names is Tarlith Vhal Sereth, the recluse.
  • Hesren Vesh's full role. If the GM has played him as the "junior agent who did not know what his seniors were doing" in Phase 2, Phase 3 confirms that read. He is a witness for Phase 4 if used carefully.

Tactical Map

Hardby — city map. Old city south, new city north, the docks where Black Cog Lane runs into the harbor.

Arrival under the Official Delegation. The herald in oxblood-and-grey rides ahead; under the gate, the old tower-and-three-waves clearing-mark watches.

Read aloud as the party reaches Hardby. Use this version only if they chose the Official Delegation approach in Phase 2.

You see Hardby a full half-day before the city itself. The road from the west bends along the Sheldomar's last broad meander, and the harbor's masts crowd the horizon like a winter forest the wind has stripped of leaves. The smoke that hangs above the city is darker than Loftwick's — coal smoke, tar smoke, the smell of ships being repaired and ships being broken up for the same lumber.

You arrive on the south road in a column. The Yeomanry councilman who lent his name rides at the head; two clerks behind him; you behind them; a Yeomanry guard escort behind you. Three hundred yards from the West Gate, a Hardby herald in oxblood-and-grey livery rides out to meet you. He bows to the councilman the small bow Hardby uses for foreign accredited visitors and explains, with the careful politeness of a man whose afternoon you have ruined, that the Gynarchy has prepared formal accommodations at the Hall of Salt and Tide on Whalemarket Quay, and that audiences with appropriate parties have been arranged for the day after tomorrow.

The day after tomorrow.

The councilman thanks him. The councilman thanks him again. The councilman says nothing for a long time after the herald rides ahead.

Inside the West Gate, two strangers in unfamiliar livery are waiting beside the duty officer. They take note of every face in your party in the manner of clerks who have been told to take note. They do not speak. They do not bow. They write.

Above the gate's inner arch, set in stone older than the gate itself, an old Aerdi clearing-mark has been chiseled into the lintel: a tower above three waves. It is faint with weather, very old. It is the same mark as the silver coin in your pack.

What's true on this approach

  • The party has legal accreditation. They cannot be detained or harassed by the Hardby city watch without diplomatic incident. This is genuinely useful and should not be undersold.
  • The conspiracy has had two weeks to prepare since the credentials were filed. Witnesses have been moved or silenced. Tamsin Moraven's body was recovered three days ago from the harbor. The party will hear of it within the first day from the watch sergeant on rotation at the West Gate.
  • Veska Maelan has fled Hardby. Her notary office on Whalemarket Quay is shuttered. A former apprentice of hers — a young scribe named Hella Voren (no relation to the Voren on Haskur's false letter; common Hardby surname) — works at the Quay's clearing-house and may be approached privately. She has Veska's notes, which she promised to deliver to anyone who could speak the agreed phrase: "A tower above three waves."
  • Tarsh has been moved upcountry "for his health." The party gets to him only through paper trails — letters of credit, hire records, the chains of intermediaries that will eventually let them name him. They do not confront him in person on this approach. They can, however, expose him publicly through the documents.
  • Cindren & Vhal Hardby will receive the official delegation as required by Aerdi commercial law. Senior management (a junior partner named Mira Cindren, Veshen Cindren's cousin) will be polite and prepared and will give nothing useful. The vault is closed for "routine audit." The chops the party wants to compare are unavailable.
  • The investigation here is document-driven. What the party recovers will be enough to prove conspiracy in Phase 4 court, but they will leave Hardby without the satisfaction of having stopped anything in the moment. Tamsin's smuggled last letter (passed by Hella Voren) is the emotional weight.

The councilman

The Yeomanry councilman who heads the delegation — name him whatever fits your campaign; default Councilman Ortwell Brane (yes, Phase 1's faction-1 closure-Council figure, who was politically maneuvered into accepting this duty and is privately furious) — is a problem and an asset. He is Caelith's political enemy and he resents the assignment. He is also a competent civic functionary who will not break protocol. Use him as friction the party must work around: he will not attend any meeting they organize off-protocol; he will report what he sees back to his faction. The party may decide to brief him selectively or not at all.

Arrival under the Commercial Cover. The South Gate guard waves the wool-trade delegation through without a second look.

Read aloud as the party reaches Hardby. Use this version only if they chose the Commercial Cover approach in Phase 2.

You enter Hardby through the South Gate at sundown, on the second day of riding. Your hired packhorses carry samples of Yeomanry wool and fleece in canvas bales; your saddlebags carry the modest letters of introduction Caelith arranged for you with two Hardby wool buyers and a Salters' Quarter dyer. You are a small private wool-trade delegation. The story is plausible. The papers are real.

The South Gate guard glances at the bales, glances at the lead rider's papers, asks two perfunctory questions about the spring fleece market, and waves the party through. He has done this a thousand times. He will not remember you tomorrow.

Beyond the gate the Salters' Quarter opens in a long low slope of warehouses, herring-smoke houses, and the kind of inns that lodge merchants for two coppers more than the cheaper inns and twice the cleanness. The streets smell of sea, salt, smoke, and tar. Bells from the harbor's clock tower mark the half-hour. Children selling hot bread call out two names — one for the day's loaves, one for tomorrow's, which they will call all night to mean the same.

You make for the address Trina gave you: a tall stone-fronted townhouse on a quiet lane off Rope Walk, where a small brass plate by the door reads simply VELL.

The man who opens the door has dark curls graying at the temples, a poet's clean unringed hands, and the kind of careful smile that has, over years, learned to look like welcome whether or not it actually is. He looks at the party for one long moment, then steps back and opens the door fully.

"Mistress Alvere wrote to me about you," he says. "Come in. The wine is good and the kettle is just on. I have been hoping you would arrive before the firm noticed."

What's true on this approach

  • The party has plausible cover and useful papers. Most Hardby civic officials and most C&V Hardby staff will accept them at face value as a wool-trade visit. The cover holds for as long as the party doesn't break it.
  • The conspiracy has had about a week of post-Pressure-Clock alarm but has not confirmed the party is in Hardby specifically. They suspect; they don't know.
  • Tamsin Moraven is alive but has been moved. Held in a coastal estate two days east of Hardby (the Whitemoor Estate, a former wine-house owned through three shell companies). Recoverable with a side trip and competent reconnaissance.
  • Veska Maelan is alive in Hardby. Cautious. The party meets her on her terms — at her notary office in business hours, with Caelith's letter of introduction visible. She will help once she has read the letter and concluded the party can be trusted.
  • Castrian Vell is the party's host and primary social network. He keeps a salon every third evening; he can introduce them to nearly anyone in the merchants' quarter without the introduction reading suspicious. He is genuinely warm and genuinely talkative; manage his cups, and he can open doors. Do not embarrass him in his own house.
  • Tarsh is alive in Hardby and reachable. Confronted in person at his usual evening haunt — see Tarsh dossier.
  • Cindren & Vhal Hardby will receive a wool-trade visit politely. They will not open the vault on a wool inquiry, but a sustained-pretext visit can yield observation: who works there, what hours, where the senior clerk Solen Mereth keeps his correspondence cabinet.

Castrian's house

Two-story townhouse, modest by Hardby merchant standards but rich by Yeomanry. Sitting room downstairs (where the party will sit and drink wine and talk); a study off the back where Castrian writes; three guest bedchambers upstairs (he can house the full party tightly); a small kitchen run by his housekeeper, a widow named Sarro Pell who has worked for him eleven years and notices everything.

Castrian's salon evenings (every third night): four to six guests, drawn from poets, sculptors, two minor merchants, one retired ship's captain, one Gynarchy under-clerk who attends mostly for the wine. The party can attend as "Mistress Alvere's wool friends from the west" without raising eyebrows.

Arrival under Quiet Pursuit. Hardby smells of low tide; the harbor clock rings six and the firm has not yet thought to look for trouble.

Read aloud as the party reaches Hardby. Use this version only if they chose the Quiet Pursuit approach in Phase 2.

You came hard, by fast horses and short rests, and Hardby comes up at you sooner than expected — a stack of grey roofs above the river haze on the morning of the fourth day. Your saddlebags are light. Your false-name papers are still folded in their wax. You have not yet been seen by anyone who would know what to look for.

The South Gate guard takes your false papers, asks no questions, hands them back. You are travelers. You could be anyone. He will not remember you and there is, this morning, no one waiting at the gate to recognize you.

Hardby in the early morning smells of low tide, fish smoked overnight, and chimney coal that has not yet caught the day. The streets at this hour are mostly stevedores, fishwives, dock-warden shift changes. A bell from the harbor's clock tower rings six. A boy selling hot bread for the morning catches your eye, calls a price, and is gone past you.

You have one address Caelith gave you and one phrase: "A tower above three waves." The address is a notary office on Whalemarket Quay. The phrase is the door, if she is willing to open it. Beyond that you carry only what you brought.

It is the third day after Hesren Vesh's recall message left Loftwick. The firm is just beginning to wonder why no one from Loftwick has yet arrived to make trouble. They have not yet thought to look for trouble that arrived on its own.

What's true on this approach

  • The party has surprise. The conspiracy does not yet know they are in Hardby. They have, conservatively, three to five days before the firm's senior staff put together that the bad news from Loftwick has walked into Hardby on its own feet.
  • No diplomatic protection. False-name papers will hold for casual checks but will not hold under any formal scrutiny. If you are arrested, Caelith cannot reach you.
  • Tamsin Moraven is alive and still in Hardby, held in a warehouse on Black Cog Lane in the harbor district. Live recovery is possible if the party moves quickly — within three days of arrival is comfortable, within five is tight, beyond a week she is moved.
  • Veska Maelan is alive, in her notary office on Whalemarket Quay, fully cooperative. She has been waiting for someone to come; Caelith's last cipher to her warned her to expect quiet visitors. The phrase opens her door immediately.
  • Tarsh is alive and unprepared. The party can confront him in person at his usual evening haunt (see Tarsh dossier) and have the easiest version of that confrontation; he is not yet braced.
  • Cindren & Vhal Hardby has not yet hardened. The senior clerk Solen Mereth keeps his correspondence cabinet at the office; the vault opens for routine business. A burglary or a sustained reconnaissance can yield the documents the party needs.
  • If the party is recognized at any point during their first three days, the firm escalates immediately and Phase 3 collapses inward. Watch sergeants are bought, mercenary squads on retainer become available to the firm, and the party loses its surprise advantage. The Pressure Clock for Phase 3 (see Departure) advances by 2 segments on recognition.

The address

Caelith's address: Maelan, Notary & Trade-Clearing, Whalemarket Quay, Hardby. Above the door, painted in faded gold on dark wood: a small open scroll and a balance scale.

The phrase: "A tower above three waves." Spoken at her threshold by anyone who is not on her usual visitor list. She will repeat it back if she is alone in the office; if she does not repeat it, she is not alone, and the party should leave and try later.

Caelith stays in Loftwick. He is not present in Phase 3 in person. He corresponds with the party by sealed post via a small chain of trusted couriers, two to three days each way. The GM may use these letters as transitions between scenes, or as the "morning post" beat between days of investigation.

Letter 1 — within two days of arrival

If you are reading this, the courier I trusted with it has reached you, which is itself information.

Three things since you left.

First — Reln Pollow [or whoever segment-3 of the Pressure Clock removed; adjust to taste] is no longer with us. I should have moved him sooner. I will remember.

Second — the Council motion of Civic Reconciliation Closure has been filed by Brane's faction and is on track to pass within the fortnight. The Audit Hall withdraws clerical support in three days. After that, anything you send back to me must come privately, not through any office I notionally control.

Third — Trina has visited the Moraven household. Vellin Moraven is well enough. Ailen, if you have located her in Hardby, may have already received Trina's letter telling her to come home. Please do nothing that would cause her to be on the road during a moment when the firm is considering whether her continued health serves them.

I will write again when there is news. Look for the seal you saw on the workbench.

— C.

Letter 2 — about a week into Phase 3

News, in two parts.

The clerk in my office. I have a name. I will not write it here. I am sending a separate sealed pouch by a separate courier; if you receive both within three days of each other, open the pouch in private, read the name, and burn it. If you receive only this letter and not the pouch within five days of this letter, my courier is dead and the pouch is in someone else's hands. In that case, do not act on any communication that claims to be from me until I write you again with the seal you saw on the workbench.

I have also written to a person I trust in Hardby about whom I have never told you, and may not. If a Gynarchy under-clerk named Zoria Weis approaches you privately and uses the phrase "the autumn ledger," she is mine. She is not, however, a friend; she is a debt I am calling. Do not over-trust her, and do not say anything to her you would not want eventually said to the Gynarchy proper.

Find Tamsin alive if you can. I am sorry I cannot say that more usefully.

— C.

Letter 3 — toward the end of Phase 3

If you are reading this, you have not yet left for Rel Astra.

Two things.

First — whatever document you have recovered that names Tarlith Vhal Sereth (and I trust you have one, or are about to), do not bring it to Rel Astra. Send it back to me through the channel we have established, and carry only a copy. The original will be safer in the Audit Hall — or, more truthfully, in Trina's strongroom, where the Audit Hall cannot easily reach it — than in your saddlebags on the eastern road.

Second — there is a small matter of the harbor council seats. I have been reading the older Aerdi commentaries on the Concordat of the Bay. The harbor commission of Rel Astra is composed of seven members; under the Concordat, three of these seats are filled by old-house appointment that the new throne, when seated, must confirm. The conspirators do not need to seat all three. They only need to seat the three that vote together first. I am writing this in case it is useful when you are inside the city and someone says the word "commission" and you would rather not have to look puzzled.

Travel safely. Trina sends her quiet regards to Alicia.

— C.
